title: FTS Statement on HB1236 and a Suggested Amendment
author: James Silberman
orientation: Free the States endorses Oklahoma HB1236 (a bill recognizing the legislature's right to review and refuse unconstitutional federal acts) but argues it is fatally narrow because it omits the federal judiciary, and proposes specific amendment language that would extend nullification authority to federal court rulings such as Roe v. Wade.
audiences: Pro-life Republicans deferential to a 'conservative' SCOTUS
Abolitionists pressing for immediate state defiance of Roe v. Wade

questions.question: Does Oklahoma actually need HB1236 in order to defy unconstitutional federal acts?
questions.answer: No. Free the States argues the bill is helpful for making the right explicit, but the underlying authority already exists because the federal government is itself a creation of the States and cannot be the final judge of its own powers.
questions.quote: Before addressing any specifics of the bill, it is crucial to note that the passage of this bill is not actually necessary in order to defy evil or illegal federal usurpations. The federal government is a creation of the States, and the US Constitution is a pact made between the states. It is therefore absurd to claim that the federal government possesses the authority to determine the extent of its own power over the States.

questions.question: On what grounds, beyond the Constitution, does Free the States justify nullification?
questions.answer: On the Jeffersonian claim that nullification is a natural right rooted in the duty to refuse evil, a duty binding even on inferior authorities receiving orders from superiors.
questions.quote: Further, more foundational than any Constitutional argument is the fact that nullification – as Thomas Jefferson put it – is a *natural right*. Put simply, nullification is refusing to do evil. The federal government is not superior to the States, but even if it were, inferior authorities have a right and duty to resist evil orders from a superior.

questions.question: Why does Free the States invoke the Nuremberg trials when discussing state compliance with federal orders?
questions.audience: Pro-life Republicans deferential to a 'conservative' SCOTUS
questions.answer: Because the 'just following orders' defense was rejected at Nuremberg, demonstrating there is a higher law that voids any human command to commit atrocity, including orders that participate in abortion.
questions.quote: Many were executed and rightly so because there is a law higher than man's law. The Declaration of Independence refers to it as the law 'of nature and nature's God.' Any government order to murder the innocent is null by its nature because it is in violation of the highest law. For this reason, 'I'm just following orders' or 'I'm just following the law' is no legitimate defense for those who play a part in atrocities.

questions.question: What is the central deficiency Free the States identifies in HB1236 as passed by the Oklahoma House?
questions.answer: The bill omits the federal judiciary from the list of bodies whose acts Oklahoma may review and reject, even though courts have produced the worst constitutional abuses.
questions.quote: As passed by the House, HB1236 only recognizes the right of Oklahoma to review a federal executive order, agency rule, or congressional act. The worst unconstitutional abuses have come from the federal judiciary, yet HB1236 does not recognize any right to defy the judiciary.

questions.question: Why does Silberman reject the assumption that a 'conservative' Supreme Court will fix federal overreach?
questions.audience: Pro-life Republicans deferential to a 'conservative' SCOTUS
questions.answer: Because Republican appointees have repeatedly produced progressive rulings (he cites Bostock and a list of justices), and even an actually conservative court has shown no intention of undoing decades of tyrannical precedent.
questions.quote: As recently as last year, our "conservative supreme court" ruled in favor of the most deranged leftist delusions in *Bostock v. Clayton County* by ruling that homosexuality and transgenderism are protected classes. John Roberts is a reliable progressive vote, Neil Gorsuch is all over the place, and Amy Coney Barrett and Brett Kavanaugh are extremely timid after their brutal confirmation processes.

questions.question: What unspoken political reason does Silberman attribute to legislators for excluding federal court rulings from HB1236?
questions.audience: Abolitionists pressing for immediate state defiance of Roe v. Wade
questions.answer: Because acknowledging the right to defy federal court rulings would force them to acknowledge the right to defy Roe v. Wade, which Oklahoma's legislature has spent five years refusing to do.
questions.quote: Second, recognizing the right to defy unconstitutional actions of the federal judiciary would mean they are recognizing the right to defy *Roe v. Wade*. By refusing to support the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, the pro-life Republican dominated Oklahoma legislature has made abundantly clear over the past five years that they desire to keep the State submissive to the genocidal opinions offered in *Roe v. Wade*.

questions.question: How does Silberman characterize the standard legislative excuse for not abolishing abortion in Oklahoma?
questions.audience: Abolitionists pressing for immediate state defiance of Roe v. Wade
questions.answer: He calls it the Nuremberg Defense applied to SCOTUS, claiming Oklahoma legislators present themselves as helpless functionaries merely following the Supreme Court's orders.
questions.quote: When pressed about their refusal to abolish abortion, the most common objection of Oklahoma legislators is the Nuremberg Defense. "Beifel from the supreme court est beifel" they claim. When the Oklahoma State government approves of the mass murder of preborn children, they're simply following the orders of the supreme court and can do no other.

questions.question: What concrete amendment does Free the States propose to Section 1 of HB1236?
questions.answer: Inserting 'federal court ruling' and 'ruling' into the list of federal acts the legislature and Attorney General may review and seek to have declared unconstitutional.
questions.quote: "The Legislature may review any executive order issued by the President of the United States, **federal court ruling,** federal agency rule or federal congressional action to determine the constitutionality of the order, rule or action. Upon recommendation from the Legislature, the Attorney General of this state shall review the order, **ruling,** rule or action by the federal government to determine the constitutionality of the order, **ruling,** rule or action and whether the state should seek an exemption from the application of the order, **ruling,** rule or action or seek to have the order, **ruling,** rule or action declared unconstitutional."

questions.question: What action does Free the States call on Oklahomans to take at the April 19th capitol rally?
questions.answer: Attend the rally and personally lobby their senators to amend HB1236 so that it includes the right to defy unconstitutional federal court rulings.
questions.quote: We encourage Oklahomans to attend tomorrow's rally at the Capitol and, while meeting with Senators, to request HB1236 be amended to include the defiance of unconstitutional federal court rulings.

questions.question: How does Silberman frame Senate President Greg Treat's role in the HB1236 fight?
questions.answer: He labels Treat 'Oklahoma's leading federal bootlicker' and previews a forthcoming response to what he characterizes as Treat's ignorant historical and constitutional arguments against the bill.
questions.quote: Unsurprisingly, none other than Oklahoma's leading federal bootlicker Greg Treat has published a statement opposing HB1236. His stated reasons are incredibly ignorant, even for him. We will have a write-up responding to his ludicrous historical and constitutional incoherencies up on our blog tomorrow, so make sure to check back then!

related.title: Greg Treat's Mind-Numbingly Ignorant Opposition to HB1236
related.reason: The promised follow-up piece directly answering Treat's stated objections to HB1236, written one day later.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: Provides the historical defense of nullification this article assumes, rebutting the standard 'nullifiers were the bad guys' narrative.

related.title: Contrary to Treat's Media Tour, SB13 is Not a Secession Bill
related.reason: Earlier round of the same Greg-Treat-vs-nullification fight, showing his consistent pattern of conflating defiance with secession.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Foundational explainer of the doctrine HB1236 is built on, useful background for any reader new to the concept.

related.title: Crime Against Humanity
related.reason: The article Silberman quotes at length for the Nuremberg Defense argument grounding his case against 'just following SCOTUS' reasoning.

related.title: Arkansas SB6: "Pleading" When We Should Be Defying
related.reason: Parallel critique of state pro-life bills that beg the federal courts rather than defy them, the same posture HB1236's omission perpetuates.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Companion piece confronting pro-life legislators in another state who, like Oklahoma's, refuse to act on the very nullification theory HB1236 codifies.
